questions.question: How clearly have you or your leadership thought about the role of digital tools or systems in growing your business?

options.O1: We haven’t really thought about using digital to grow the business yet.
options.O2: We know it’s important, but haven’t connected it clearly to our business goals
options.O3: We’re starting to think seriously about it and exploring specific actions.
options.O4: Using digital tools is already part of how we plan and run the business.

insights.I1: Not thinking about digital early can make it harder to compete, especially as others move faster.
insights.I2: Knowing it’s important is a good start, but without a plan, opportunities for growth or cost savings may be missed.
insights.I3: You’re moving in the right direction—turning ideas into clear actions will unlock real results.
insights.I4: You’re using digital to drive your growth and are well-placed to move faster than your competitors.

questions.question: How involved are you—or your team—in actively moving digital transformation forward in your business?

options.O1: Digital topics rarely come up when decisions are made
options.O2: We acknowledge digital is important but haven’t taken direct steps yet
options.O3: We’ve started exploring digital seriously, either personally or engaged and external expert
options.O4: We regularly drive digital actions—discuss, review, and make decisions to move forward

insights.I1: Lack of leadership involvement often results in delays and missed digital opportunities.
insights.I2: Without consistent action, digital ideas may stay stuck at the discussion stage
insights.I3: Taking ownership and assigning responsibility builds momentum
insights.I4: Active leadership ensures digital plans turn into results

questions.question: Has your company articulated and communicated a clear vision for digital transformation?

options.O1: There’s no defined digital vision
options.O2: Some ideas exist, but not clearly shared
options.O3: A vision exists and is internally communicated to key managers
options.O4: We have a compelling digital vision, widely communicated and owned

insights.I1: Absence of vision leads to fragmented initiatives and poor team alignment.
insights.I2: Vision exists in silos; not widely communicated.
insights.I3: Team knows the vision but not yet fully aligned.
insights.I4: Vision is clear, shared, and mobilizing teams toward transformation.

questions.question: How is your company managing digital transformation initiatives at a governance level?

options.O1: No formal ownership or tracking structure in place
options.O2: Occasional reviews or ad-hoc responsibility by operations/IT
options.O3: A designated leader/team tracks digital initiatives periodically
options.O4: A formal governance structure exists with KPIs, review cadences, and accountability

insights.I1: No governance risks duplication, scope creep, and lack of accountability.
insights.I2: Responsibility exists but no formal tracking.
insights.I3: Governance is evolving; reviews happen irregularly.
insights.I4: Governance is structured with KPIs and decision accountability.

questions.group: External Awareness (Industry, Policy, Tech, Competition, Customers)
questions.question: How aware are you of changes happening in your industry—like new customer demands, technologies, or competitor moves?

options.O1: We don’t track changes in the industry actively.
options.O2: We hear about trends but don’t follow them closely.
options.O3: We pay attention to trends and adjust some things based on them.
options.O4: We actively track trends and use them to guide our business decisions.

insights.I1: Staying unaware of changes can make your business vulnerable to disruption
insights.I2: Knowing about trends but not acting on them puts you behind faster competitors.
insights.I3: Following trends helps you stay relevant and avoid surprises.
insights.I4: Proactive awareness helps you lead change and gain a competitive edge.

questions.group: External Awareness (Industry, Policy, Tech, Competition, Customers)
questions.question: How do you understand what your customers need or expect, especially as things change?

options.O1: We don’t have a structured way to gather customer feedback.
options.O2: We rely on informal chats or complaints to understand customers.
options.O3: We ask for feedback sometimes and make changes accordingly.
options.O4: We have a structured Voice of Customer (VoC) or market intelligence process

insights.I1: Without customer insight, you risk building things people don’t want.
insights.I2: Relying on complaints means reacting too late.
insights.I3: Listening to customers helps improve service and loyalty.
insights.I4: Customer feedback, when used well, drives growth and innovation

questions.group: External Awareness (Industry, Policy, Tech, Competition, Customers)
questions.question: How actively does your company track and evaluate emerging technologies relevant to your industry?

options.O1: We don’t' follow techology updates or know what's available
options.O2: We hear about new tools but haven't explored them for ourselves
options.O3: We look at what others are using and try out relevant tools
options.O4: We actively explore new tech and assess how it can help us

insights.I1: Ignoring technology can cause your business to fall behind.
insights.I2: Second-hand info limits your ability to choose the right tools
insights.I3: Exploring tech gives you early advantage and confidence to act
insights.I4: Being tech-forward lets you outperform competitors and attract better customers

questions.group: External Awareness (Industry, Policy, Tech, Competition, Customers)
questions.question: How prepared is your company to deal with regulatory and policy changes (e.g., ESG, EPR, GST, global certifications)?

options.O1: We react to changes only when they become urgent
options.O2: We react when others in the industry start making changes.
options.O3: We monitor changes and integrate them into operations
options.O4: We anticipate regulations and align strategy proactively

insights.I1: Being unprepared for rules can cause fines, rework, or lost business.
insights.I2: Following others delays your ability to act confidently
insights.I3: Planning ahead helps avoid disruptions and improves compliance.
insights.I4: Proactive alignment with policy creates trust and first-mover advantage.

questions.group: External Awareness (Industry, Policy, Tech, Competition, Customers)
questions.question: How clear is your understanding of your position relative to peers in terms of pricing, capabilities, and digital maturity?

options.O1: We haven't done any comparison or benchmarking
options.O2: We have a general sense, but not detailed insight
options.O3: We periodically assess competitors and compare KPIs
options.O4: We track upcoming changes and stay ahead in compliance and planning

insights.I1: You risk stagnation if unaware of peers' digital and cost benchmarks.
insights.I2: Benchmarking is informal or backward-looking.
insights.I3: Peer comparisons are structured but infrequent.
insights.I4: Real-time benchmarking guides strategic moves.

questions.group: External Awareness (Industry, Policy, Tech, Competition, Customers)
questions.question: Does your digital strategy focus on enhancing your company‚Äôs unique positioning or market edge?

options.O1: We aren't using digital tools in a way that sets us apart
options.O2: We use some tools, but they don't offer a major advantage
options.O3: Our tools give us faster service, lower cost, or better quality
options.O4: Our digital approach clearly gives us a competitive edge

insights.I1: Lack of clarity in USP undermines brand and customer loyalty.
insights.I2: Some initiatives build on USPs but lack strategy.
insights.I3: Differentiation is selectively reinforced by digital tools.
insights.I4: DT is a driver of brand, performance, and market uniqueness.

questions.question: Does your organization have a formal digital transformation roadmap or phased execution plan?

options.O1: We haven't created a plan or discussed this yet
options.O2: We have had some ideas, but nothing structured or written down
options.O3: We have listed a few things we want to do and are getting started
options.O4: We have a clear, phased plan with timelines and goals

insights.I1: No roadmap = reactive execution and wasted digital spend.
insights.I2: Unclear direction often leads to wasted time and scattered efforts
insights.I3: Even a simple plan helps you move forward with confidence.
insights.I4: A clear roadmap keeps everyone aligned and focused on results

questions.question: How well are digital initiatives mapped to measurable business outcomes (e.g., cost savings, new revenue, market expansion)?

options.O1: We are doing a few digital things, but not sure their impact on the business results
options.O2: Some tools or projects support our goals, but not all and sub par results
options.O3: Most things we are doing support goals like cost saving or service improvement
options.O4: Every digital action we take is tied to clear business outcomes

insights.I1: Disconnected digital work rarely delivers value.
insights.I2: Partial alignment means some effort goes unnoticed or underused.
insights.I3: Tying work to goals improves focus and accountability
insights.I4: Full alignment turns digital into a growth engine, not a side project

questions.question: Do you use structured tools or models (e.g., SWOT, Industry 4.0 maturity, BMC) to guide your digital strategy?

options.O1: We don't use any structured methods to guide decisions
options.O2: We talk through ideas but don't use any specific tool or template
options.O3: We have tried using some frameworks for planning and review
options.O4: We regularly use planning tools to guide digital actions and choices

insights.I1: Without structure, digital decisions are harder to track or repeat.
insights.I2: Unstructured planning can lead to missed steps or unclear priorities.
insights.I3: Even basic tools help you bring clarity and focus to your efforts.
insights.I4: Using structured tools improves decision-making and planning speed.

questions.question: How does your organization allocate financial and operational resources for digital initiatives?

options.O1: We haven’t planned any resources for digital work yet.
options.O2: We invest in digital only when there’s an urgent need.
options.O3: We have some budget or time set aside for digital each year.
options.O4: We treat digital like any key function—with a clear plan and resources.

insights.I1: Without committed resources, digital work often stays stuck.
insights.I2: Budgeting is reactive or fragmented. Only investing in emergencies delays real progress.
insights.I3: Planning ahead makes it easier to follow through on digital actions.
insights.I4: Dedicated resources ensure consistent and effective digital transformation.

questions.question: How ready are you—financially and mentally—to invest in digital transformation?

options.O1: We are focused on daily operations; digital can wait
options.O2: We can invest small amounts when something urgent comes up
options.O3: We are financially stable and ready to support digital efforts
options.O4: We are fully committed and actively investing in digital for long-term growth

insights.I1: No readiness means missed chances to improve or scale.
insights.I2: Small steps help, but may not be enough for major gains.
insights.I3: Financial readiness allows you to move faster than others.
insights.I4: Committed businesses turn digital into a real competitive advantage.

questions.question: How do you currently track the status of production or work orders across the shop floor?

options.O1: We rely on paper records or verbal updates
options.O2: We use spreadsheets or informal methods
options.O3: We track jobs digitally, but only at key points
options.O4: We have live dashboards or tools that show real-time job status

insights.I1: Relying on manual methods causes delays, miscommunication, and limited visibility.
insights.I2: Spreadsheets help slightly but often lead to delays and incomplete insights.
insights.I3: Tracking some key points digitally helps, but lack of full integration causes blind spots.
insights.I4: Live dashboards ensure full visibility and enable proactive interventions.

questions.question: How is your team monitoring and reporting key production metrics like output, downtime, or rework?

options.O1: We don’t track these metrics formally
options.O2: We capture some numbers manually or through verbal reports
options.O3: We track key metrics using Excel or basic systems
options.O4: We track performance automatically and review it regularly

insights.I1: No consistent performance tracking leads to blind spots and poor productivity decisions.
insights.I2: Manual logs are effort-intensive and miss real-time trends.
insights.I3: Basic tracking shows trends but lacks automation for timely action.
insights.I4: Real-time monitoring supports continuous improvement and higher productivity.

questions.question: How do you manage your inventory levels across raw materials, WIP, and finished goods?

options.O1: It’s mostly based on physical checks and team memory
options.O2: We maintain stock lists in Excel or registers
options.O3: We use software to update and monitor stock periodically
options.O4: We have a live inventory system that syncs with production and sales

insights.I1: Relying on memory and manual checks causes stockouts, overstocking, and inefficiency.
insights.I2: Excel-based tracking is fragile and requires constant checking.
insights.I3: Periodic updates help, but misalignment with demand/sales causes waste.
insights.I4: Integrated live systems reduce losses, optimize inventory, and increase agility.

questions.question: How quickly can you identify production delays or bottlenecks as they happen?

options.O1: We only know after delays occur or orders are missed
options.O2: We depend on supervisor updates to catch delays
options.O3: We can spot some issues through regular reviews
options.O4: We can see delays in real time and act immediately

insights.I1: Delays go unnoticed until they disrupt delivery or customer satisfaction.
insights.I2: Supervisor updates rely on individual judgment and lag in reporting.
insights.I3: Delayed detection of issues still causes avoidable inefficiencies.
insights.I4: Immediate visibility of bottlenecks enables rapid resolution and smoother operations.

questions.question: How connected are your suppliers in terms of sharing data like orders, deliveries, or invoices?

options.O1: Everything is handled manually via calls or WhatsApp
options.O2: We exchange documents over email but not in real time
options.O3: Some suppliers use basic systems to share updates
options.O4: Most key suppliers are digitally connected to us in real time

insights.I1: Manual communication with vendors creates inefficiencies and increases error risk.
insights.I2: Non-integrated data exchange with vendors leads to inefficiencies.
insights.I3: Digital updates from suppliers help, but lack of structure limits scalability.
insights.I4: Real-time vendor integration improves speed, accuracy, and lowers costs.

questions.question: How is raw material procurement planned in relation to production needs or customer demand?

options.O1: We order materials based on past experience or urgency
options.O2: We track consumption manually and order when levels fall
options.O3: We forecast needs and plan purchases weekly or monthly
options.O4: We use systems that link demand, inventory, and procurement in real time

insights.I1: Urgent or guess-based procurement leads to material shortages or overbuying.
insights.I2: Manual tracking of consumption creates lags and missed planning windows.
insights.I3: Forecasting helps planning but delays in updates reduce effectiveness.
insights.I4: Smart procurement systems ensure just-in-time supply and reduce waste.

questions.question: Can you track incoming raw materials and outgoing deliveries in real time?

options.O1: No, we rely on manual tracking and phone calls
options.O2: We record movements after they happen
options.O3: We update movement data daily or via software
options.O4: We can track all material movements live through a system

insights.I1: Manual tracking causes poor coordination and delays in raw material or product movement.
insights.I2: Recording after-the-fact limits visibility and response effectiveness.
insights.I3: Daily updates improve visibility but delay response to fast changes.
insights.I4: Live tracking enhances logistics, customer service, and internal coordination.

questions.question: How structured is your vendor management process—from selection to performance tracking?

options.O1: We don't have a defined vendor selection or performance evaluation process.
options.O2: Vendors are chosen based on price or urgency, with basic records maintained manually.
options.O3: We use standard criteria for vendor selection and track performance in Excel or offline tools
options.O4: We follow a structured, digital vendor lifecycle management process with integrated scorecards, alerts, and reviews

insights.I1: Lack of structured vendor management leads to inconsistent quality, hidden risks, and missed cost-saving opportunities.
insights.I2: Ad-hoc vendor decisions result in reactive firefighting and unreliable supply, impacting production schedules and margins.
insights.I3: Basic performance tracking helps, but without automation and analytics, insights remain limited and hard to scale.
insights.I4: ntegrated vendor management improves quality, reduces risks, ensures compliance, and enables strategic sourcing decisions.

questions.question: How are repetitive tasks like shift handovers, quality checks, or logbooks managed?

options.O1: They are done manually using paper and verbal updates
options.O2: Some are recorded in Excel or WhatsApp, but not consistently
options.O3: Most tasks have a digital process or checklist in place
options.O4: All routine tasks are automated or digitized across departments

insights.I1: Manual and verbal task execution risks errors, misalignment, and loss of accountability.
insights.I2: Inconsistent digital records reduce repeatability and transparency.
insights.I3: Digital checklists improve consistency, but gaps reduce effectiveness.
insights.I4: Automation improves speed, traceability, and operational resilience.

questions.question: Do different teams (production, planning, stores, etc.) work with shared data or tools?

options.O1: Each team works separately using their own records
options.O2: Some teams share updates through email or meetings
options.O3: We use shared Excel sheets or tools to collaborate
options.O4: Teams access the same system and work on shared real-time data

insights.I1: Isolated records create silos, slowing decisions and reducing team synergy.
insights.I2: Partial sharing of updates leads to gaps and inefficiencies in collaboration.
insights.I3: Shared tools improve collaboration, but fragmentation still exists.
insights.I4: Unified platforms enable real-time collaboration and agile decision-making.

questions.question: Do you measure and improve process efficiency (cycle time, scrap rate, throughput)?

options.O1: We don’t track efficiency regularly
options.O2: We have rough estimates but no regular analysis
options.O3: We track some process metrics and try to improve them
options.O4: We measure efficiency across all key processes and improve actively

insights.I1: Lack of efficiency data results in resource wastage and lost improvement opportunities.
insights.I2: Rough estimates cannot support systematic performance improvement.
insights.I3: Metrics allow limited process improvement, but broader insights are missed.
insights.I4: Real-time insights boost process throughput, quality, and cost efficiency.

questions.question: How do you manage and record product quality checks?

options.O1: Quality checks are done manually and filed in registers
options.O2: We use Excel or Word to record results occasionally
options.O3: Most checks are recorded digitally and reviewed later
options.O4: We use digital tools to record, track, and analyze quality trends

insights.I1: Manual quality checks lead to inconsistent standards and poor traceability.
insights.I2: Infrequent digital records limit actionable insights.
insights.I3: Digital reviews help, but without automation, quality lags go unnoticed.
insights.I4: Continuous tracking improves product quality and audit readiness.

questions.question: Are your compliance activities (ISO, audits, safety checks) documented digitally?

options.O1: Everything is on paper or in filing cabinets
options.O2: Some records are scanned or saved on computers
options.O3: Most compliance records are stored and tracked digitally
options.O4: All compliance processes are integrated into our system with alerts and logs

insights.I1: Paper-based compliance tracking increases audit risks and administrative burden.
insights.I2: Scanned or saved files lack automation or timely access for audits.
insights.I3: Tracking is useful but manual intervention still needed to stay compliant.
insights.I4: Digital compliance ensures readiness for audits and builds operational trust.

questions.question: How well does your operations team adapt to urgent changes—like a rush order or line breakdown?

options.O1: It takes time and disruption to handle changes
options.O2: We manage through verbal coordination or manager intervention
options.O3: We respond using flexible planning and manual tracking
options.O4: We have systems in place that help us respond quickly with minimal disruption

insights.I1: Reactive adaptation causes disruption and customer dissatisfaction.
insights.I2: Verbal coordination works for simple changes but doesn’t scale well.
insights.I3: Manual flexibility helps short term, but long-term agility suffers.
insights.I4: Responsive systems reduce downtime, increase productivity, and boost trust.

questions.question: Are you able to generate reports or insights on your operations without manual compilation?

options.O1: Reports are created manually and take time
options.O2: We use Excel templates but they require manual input
options.O3: We have software that generates reports with some effort
options.O4: Reports are auto-generated and available on demand

insights.I1: Manual report compilation delays decision-making and increases errors.
insights.I2: Templates ease effort but still require redundant manual data handling.
insights.I3: Software support helps, but partial automation limits strategic use.
insights.I4: On-demand reports enhance strategic decision-making and free up resources.

questions.question: Do you have an official website?

options.O1: No official website exists.
options.O2: A simple webpage exists but lacks updates or useful information.
options.O3: Updated catalog with inquiry
options.O4: Integrated site with SEO/analytics

insights.I1: Zero digital presence; invisible to online buyers.
insights.I2: Basic presence but not discoverable; low credibility.
insights.I3: Functional website enables some engagement, but underleveraged.
insights.I4: Optimized site builds trust, improves search visibility, and captures leads.

questions.question: Are your products/services listed online?

options.O1: Products/services not listed anywhere online.
options.O2: Listed on a niche platform
options.O3: Listed on IndiaMART/Udaan
options.O4: Listed on multiple B2B/B2C sites

insights.I1: Not listed online; no digital discovery or inbound traction.
insights.I2: Presence exists but not optimized or regularly updated.
insights.I3: Some reach and lead generation through local marketplaces.
insights.I4: Strategic listing improves reach, SEO, and multi-channel demand.

questions.question: Do you use social media for lead generation?

options.O1: Never used or implemented.
options.O2: Occasional social media posts with no lead intent.
options.O3: Regular posts but untracked
options.O4: Targeted campaigns with analytics

insights.I1: No social presence means zero digital brand visibility.
insights.I2: Irregular effort leads to low engagement and poor ROI.
insights.I3: Consistent posts create awareness but lack conversion focus.
insights.I4: Data-driven campaigns turn social into a strong lead channel.

questions.question: Do you run any digital ad campaigns?

options.O1: Never attempted or initiated.
options.O2: Digital ads were briefly tried but then discontinued.
options.O3: Occasional low-budget ads
options.O4: Actively run optimized campaigns

insights.I1: No digital leads; total reliance on offline acquisition.
insights.I2: Lack of ROI or clarity caused early abandonment.
insights.I3: Some traction, but scale and targeting are missing.
insights.I4: Optimized ads provide scale, data, and predictable growth.

questions.question: How do you track leads and follow-ups?

options.O1: Lead tracking is managed through paper notes or Excel sheets.
options.O2: A CRM or digital tool exists but is rarely used properly.
options.O3: Only a few salespeople are actively using the CRM.
options.O4: CRM used across team with automation

insights.I1: Leads lost in manual chaos; poor conversions.
insights.I2: CRM adoption is low, limiting visibility and action.
insights.I3: Partial adoption results in inconsistent performance.
insights.I4: Full CRM usage drives accountability and win rates.

questions.question: Is there a structured sales funnel or pipeline?

options.O1: No defined structure or practice in place.
options.O2: Sales funnel exists informally but not documented or tracked.
options.O3: Sales funnel has defined stages tracked manually.
options.O4: Clear stages with analytics and dashboards

insights.I1: No defined stages; inconsistent sales practices across reps.
insights.I2: Informal funnel lacks discipline or follow-through.
insights.I3: Pipeline exists but lacks data and visibility.
insights.I4: Structured funnel enables forecasting and conversion insights.

questions.question: How is after-sales feedback handled?

options.O1: No structured method for handling this aspect.
options.O2: Feedback is collected occasionally via calls or emails.
options.O3: Feedback collected via forms or emails but not integrated.
options.O4: Automated feedback & NPS integrated

insights.I1: No feedback captured; missed CX improvements.
insights.I2: Inconsistent collection limits learning from customers.
insights.I3: Data is collected but insights are rarely analyzed.
insights.I4: Automated CX insights enable retention and loyalty strategies.

questions.question: Do sales reps use digital tools?

options.O1: No digital tools; rely entirely on manual, paper-based methods.
options.O2: Sales reps use PowerPoint decks or printed catalogs.
options.O3: Tablets/mobile CRM
options.O4: Live inventory/quoting on devices

insights.I1: Sales rely on outdated methods with limited information.
insights.I2: Basic tools enable passive sharing, not active selling.
insights.I3: Mobile tools improve accuracy and confidence in sales.
insights.I4: Smart tools close deals faster with data-driven engagement.

questions.question: How do you gather market or customer insights?

options.O1: Business decisions are made solely based on intuition.
options.O2: Surveys or feedback calls are conducted occasionally.
options.O3: Google Analytics or social listening
options.O4: Multi-source insights + dashboards

insights.I1: No data, just instincts — high risk for wrong decisions.
insights.I2: Sporadic insights delay trend identification.
insights.I3: Some analytics used, but disconnected from strategy.
insights.I4: Insights drive proactive, adaptive decision-making.

questions.question: Is website/social analytics used to improve strategy?

options.O1: Never used or implemented.
options.O2: Analytics checked once, with no follow-up action.
options.O3: Analytics reviewed monthly but not acted upon.
options.O4: Actively use data for experiments

insights.I1: Blind to what's working or failing online.
insights.I2: Data ignored; no optimization path.
insights.I3: Some awareness, but underused for growth.
insights.I4: Experimentation and iteration drive performance gains.

questions.question: Do you segment your customers?

options.O1: One-size-fits-all
options.O2: Customers segmented by location or specific product line.
options.O3: Customers segmented by their sector, size, or business needs.
options.O4: Full persona-based, hyper-personalized

insights.I1: No segmentation leads to irrelevant offers and waste.
insights.I2: Some grouping exists, but not strategically used.
insights.I3: Tactical segmentation helps with targeted outreach.
insights.I4: Advanced segmentation powers conversion and loyalty.

questions.question: Do you personalize marketing campaigns?

options.O1: No defined structure or practice in place.
options.O2: Basic personalization such as using names in emails.
options.O3: Segment-based messaging
options.O4: Dynamic personalization via CRM/automation

insights.I1: Generic messaging erodes customer trust and engagement.
insights.I2: Superficial personalization; no competitive edge.
insights.I3: Segmented content improves relevance modestly.
insights.I4: Behavioral personalization boosts engagement and ROI.

questions.question: How is your digital brand perceived?

options.O1: Brand is largely unknown in digital channels.
options.O2: Minimal online brand presence without strategic positioning.
options.O3: Known within a specific niche or sector online.
options.O4: Strong brand + trust signals

insights.I1: Lack of brand presence erodes credibility.
insights.I2: Unremarkable presence blends into competition.
insights.I3: Credibility exists but limited to small circles.
insights.I4: Strong digital brand builds authority and buyer confidence.

questions.question: Do you sell your products directly to customers, or only through dealers and distributors?

options.O1: No defined structure or practice in place.
options.O2: Exploring the model but not yet implemented.
options.O3: Initial pilots for D2C or hybrid sales models underway.
options.O4: Fully functional with fulfillment

insights.I1: No D2C limits revenue and customer control.
insights.I2: Intent exists but execution is lacking.
insights.I3: Pilot models show promise but need scale.
insights.I4: D2C builds margin, loyalty, and market presence.

questions.question: Do you use digital tools like WhatsApp, chat, or online portals to support and communicate with customers?

options.O1: No defined structure or practice in place.
options.O2: Customer support handled manually via phone or email.
options.O3: Some CX technology like chat or portals are in use but limited.
options.O4: Full CX tools (chatbot, order tracking, ticketing)

insights.I1: Customer service stuck in the past.
insights.I2: Basic digital support, but not integrated or tracked.
insights.I3: Initial tech use improves responsiveness slightly.
insights.I4: Full CX suite delivers seamless experience and satisfaction.

questions.question: How are your day-to-day operations (sales, inventory, production, accounts) managed digitally?

options.O1: All records are maintained manually or in Excel
options.O2: Different software used for different functions (e.g., Tally + Excel + emails)
options.O3: Partial ERP or CRM in place, but not across departments
options.O4: Fully integrated ERP/CRM solution implemented

insights.I1: Operations are fragmented and manual, limiting visibility and efficiency.
insights.I2: Multiple tools create silos and manual reconciliation effort.
insights.I3: Digital tools exist, but lack integration slows execution.
insights.I4: Integrated systems enhance visibility, control, and scalability.

questions.question: How do your teams manage customer interactions and follow-ups?

options.O1: No structured system, tracked via calls or paper
options.O2: Customer details maintained in Excel or emails
options.O3: CRM system in use, but limited adoption
options.O4: CRM fully adopted across sales and service functions

insights.I1: Customer data is lost or inconsistently tracked.
insights.I2: Basic records exist but hard to access and use for follow-up.
insights.I3: CRM adoption is low, impacting customer experience and retention.
insights.I4: CRM enables proactive engagement and data-backed decisions.

questions.question: How do you manage after-sales service, field operations, or customer support?

options.O1: Handled manually or ad-hoc over phone calls
options.O2: Service data maintained offline or in spreadsheets
options.O3: Using digital tools but not integrated with CRM/ERP
options.O4: Digital service management tool integrated with main systems

insights.I1: After-sales support is reactive and undocumented.
insights.I2: Records exist but limit real-time service visibility.
insights.I3: Tools exist, but data is siloed from operations.
insights.I4: Integrated tools drive accountability and faster resolution.

questions.question: What kind of IT infrastructure supports your business (network, systems, etc.)?

options.O1: Basic computers and internet with frequent downtime
options.O2: Decent PCs and internet, but no structured IT plan
options.O3: Systems upgraded periodically with basic monitoring
options.O4: Robust IT setup with redundancy, monitoring, and upgrades

insights.I1: Weak infrastructure causes frequent disruptions.
insights.I2: Basic tech works but limits growth and security.
insights.I3: Some planning exists, but gaps remain in reliability.
insights.I4: Stable infrastructure improves productivity and risk control.

questions.question: How is data backed up and protected in your company?

options.O1: No backup process in place
options.O2: Ad-hoc/manual backups by individual teams
options.O3: Periodic backups automated, but not tested
options.O4: Fully automated, encrypted, and regularly tested backups

insights.I1: Data loss risk is extremely high.
insights.I2: Inconsistent backups create uncertainty and liability.
insights.I3: Some backup discipline, but recovery untested.
insights.I4: Enterprise-grade data protection ensures resilience.

questions.question: What level of cybersecurity protection do you currently use?

options.O1: No cybersecurity practices in place
options.O2: Basic antivirus and firewall setup
options.O3: Network security in place with user awareness training
options.O4: Formal cybersecurity framework with audits and compliance

insights.I1: Critical exposure to cyber threats; no protection in place.
insights.I2: Basic protection is not enough for modern threats.
insights.I3: Awareness and basic controls reduce risk, but gaps remain.
insights.I4: Comprehensive protection ensures compliance and business continuity.

questions.question: What is your current approach to hosting your software and systems?

options.O1: All software runs on office desktops or internal servers
options.O2: Some tools hosted on cloud; others on-premise
options.O3: Majority systems migrated to cloud-based platforms
options.O4: Fully cloud-native architecture across systems

insights.I1: On-prem hosting limits accessibility and increases maintenance load.
insights.I2: Hybrid setup creates complexity and inefficiency.
insights.I3: Cloud adoption improves access but needs full migration.
insights.I4: Cloud-native boosts flexibility, scale, and resilience.

questions.question: How do you analyze operational or sales data in your company?

options.O1: We don’t do any structured analysis
options.O2: We use Excel for monthly reporting
options.O3: We use dashboards from ERP/CRM for routine analysis
options.O4: Predictive dashboards and KPIs guide decisions

insights.I1: Decisions are based on intuition, not data.
insights.I2: Basic reporting lacks strategic insight or speed.
insights.I3: Dashboards help tracking, but lack forward-looking insight.
insights.I4: Predictive analytics drive informed, proactive decisions.

questions.question: How often do business decisions rely on data in your company?

options.O1: Mostly gut feel or past experience
options.O2: Data referred occasionally, mostly for finance
options.O3: Data consulted before major decisions
options.O4: Every team uses relevant data to drive decisions

insights.I1: Low data usage results in poor agility and slow decisions.
insights.I2: Finance-driven data use misses broader business insights.
insights.I3: Data-informed decisions improve consistency.
insights.I4: Data-driven culture boosts speed, alignment, and outcomes.

questions.question: What kind of tools do you use for business analysis?

options.O1: None, we rely on manual methods
options.O2: Excel/Spreadsheets with some macros or templates
options.O3: Basic BI tools like Power BI/Tableau in place
options.O4: Fully integrated BI tools with real-time data feeds

insights.I1: No tools limit visibility and reactive decision-making.
insights.I2: Spreadsheets work, but don't scale well.
insights.I3: BI tools improve insight, but integration is partial.
insights.I4: Integrated BI empowers real-time strategic execution.

questions.question: What’s your exposure to technologies like IoT, AI, AR/VR, or Digital Twin?

options.O1: Not aware or using any of these
options.O2: Aware and considering for future
options.O3: Pilot projects in progress or early adoption
options.O4: At least one emerging tech fully integrated in business

insights.I1: Zero exposure risks falling behind industry trends.
insights.I2: Awareness exists, but no active progress.
insights.I3: Early steps taken; benefits not fully realized yet.
insights.I4: Integration provides strategic advantage and differentiation.

questions.question: How do you evaluate and adopt new technologies?

options.O1: We don’t formally evaluate new tools
options.O2: We try tools recommended by vendors or peers
options.O3: Technology decisions taken after internal review
options.O4: Tech adoption based on strategic planning and ROI

insights.I1: Adoption is reactive and lacks alignment with goals.
insights.I2: Choices influenced by convenience, not needs.
insights.I3: Structured review helps balance risks and returns.
insights.I4: Strategic adoption ensures long-term value creation.

questions.question: Do you have a dedicated tech person/team or external partner to guide your IT efforts?

options.O1: No, we manage IT ourselves
options.O2: One internal person looks after basic IT
options.O3: External IT consultant or vendor manages systems
options.O4: Internal IT lead or fractional CIO with roadmap view

insights.I1: No dedicated support limits security and innovation.
insights.I2: Basic IT support handles tasks, not strategy.
insights.I3: External help provides structure but lacks internal context.
insights.I4: Strategic IT leadership drives alignment and foresight.

questions.question: How often is your company’s tech roadmap reviewed or updated?

options.O1: We don’t have a tech roadmap
options.O2: Occasionally, when something breaks or becomes outdated
options.O3: Reviewed annually or when new projects start
options.O4: Regularly reviewed with clear short- and long-term tech priorities

insights.I1: No roadmap results in scattered efforts and tech debt.
insights.I2: Tech planning is reactive, not proactive.
insights.I3: Some alignment exists but lacks agility.
insights.I4: Proactive roadmap review boosts continuity and value capture.

questions.question: How comfortable are your employees in using digital tools?

options.O1: Most employees avoid digital tools or struggle
options.O2: Mixed comfort, some training provided
options.O3: Majority are comfortable, with regular training
options.O4: Teams are confident, digitally skilled, and suggest improvements

insights.I1: Low digital comfort slows adoption and drains productivity.
insights.I2: Training exists, but gaps remain in confidence.
insights.I3: Majority participation helps sustain tools.
insights.I4: Digital fluency across teams accelerates transformation.

questions.question: How comfortable are your employees with digital tools like ERP, CRM, or production software?

options.O1: Most employees struggle or avoid using digital tools.
options.O2: Some teams are comfortable, mostly in admin or tech functions.
options.O3: Most functions are using digital tools, though some still need help.
options.O4: Employees across all functions are confident and proactive with digital tools.

insights.I1: Most employees avoid using digital tools, causing delays and manual errors.
insights.I2: Digital tools used only by admin or finance, rest rely on paper-based processes.
insights.I3: Employees across functions use digital tools with some assistance.
insights.I4: Teams are fluent with ERP/CRM and use features to drive productivity.

questions.question: Do your employees have data analysis or decision-support skills?

options.O1: Data is mostly handled by senior management or external vendors.
options.O2: A few team members can handle Excel or basic reporting.
options.O3: We have in-house capabilities for analysis, but not yet advanced.
options.O4: Data analysis is embedded in decision-making across teams.

insights.I1: Data decisions are centralized with no team member equipped for analysis.
insights.I2: Only a few staff know how to use Excel or generate basic reports.
insights.I3: Key departments use dashboards or KPIs, but analysis still reactive.
insights.I4: Teams are trained to use data proactively for decision-making and forecasting.

questions.question: Do you have cybersecurity awareness programs for your staff?

options.O1: No awareness or training exists on digital safety.
options.O2: Basic guidelines exist but not followed consistently.
options.O3: Regular sessions or reminders are shared on cyber hygiene.
options.O4: Everyone follows cybersecurity best practices; it’s part of culture.

insights.I1: Employees unaware of basic cybersecurity risks—high vulnerability.
insights.I2: Some know about risks, but practices like weak passwords are common.
insights.I3: We run ad-hoc awareness drives but still see risky behaviors.
insights.I4: Cyber safety is embedded in daily behavior; phishing awareness is high.

questions.question: How do you manage digital roles like IT, marketing tech, analytics?

options.O1: We have no dedicated roles; everything is ad hoc.
options.O2: We use freelancers or vendors for specific digital tasks.
options.O3: We have internal team members trained for some of these roles.
options.O4: We have a structured digital team or hybrid internal-external support.

insights.I1: No clear ownership for IT or digital responsibilities; high dependency on outsiders.
insights.I2: Freelancers/vendors are used, but lack integration into business goals.
insights.I3: Some team members are digitally inclined but multitask without formal roles.
insights.I4: Dedicated digital team or hybrid structure drives all tech-enabled initiatives.

questions.question: How often do employees receive formal training on new tools or systems?

options.O1: Rarely, unless absolutely necessary.
options.O2: Occasionally, depending on need or vendor support.
options.O3: We plan regular sessions for key teams.
options.O4: We follow a structured learning calendar for continuous upskilling.

insights.I1: Formal training is rare; new tools are introduced without enablement.
insights.I2: Vendors occasionally provide training but no internal ownership exists.
insights.I3: Training is scheduled for new systems, but informal learning dominates.
insights.I4: Training programs are tracked, recurring, and tailored to team needs.

questions.question: Do you allocate time during work hours for training or experimentation?

options.O1: No, we focus on daily tasks and firefighting.
options.O2: Occasionally, but it's seen as a disruption.
options.O3: We try to balance work and learning when possible.
options.O4: We set aside regular hours for learning and experimentation.

insights.I1: Workload pressure means no time is allocated to learning.
insights.I2: Learning is seen as secondary to operations, done only when free.
insights.I3: Time is occasionally reserved, but inconsistent across departments.
insights.I4: Dedicated time blocks and calendar slots exist for digital learning.

questions.question: How do you promote cross-functional exposure or digital champions?

options.O1: Teams work in silos, limited exposure across functions.
options.O2: Occasional knowledge sharing happens informally.
options.O3: We encourage job shadowing or project-based collaboration.
options.O4: We have appointed digital champions or mentors across functions.

insights.I1: Teams are siloed; no visibility into each other’s digital efforts.
insights.I2: Some departments share learnings informally but lack process.
insights.I3: We assign digital point persons, but no structure to cross-train.
insights.I4: We have active digital champions mentoring others across functions.

questions.question: How do you recognize or reward skill development among employees?

options.O1: It’s not currently part of our performance reviews.
options.O2: We verbally appreciate, but no formal system.
options.O3: We include learning goals in appraisals.
options.O4: We reward certifications, upskilling, and knowledge sharing.

insights.I1: No rewards or recognition for learning; no motivation to upskill.
insights.I2: Informal praise exists, but not linked to performance evaluation.
insights.I3: Learning is included in KPIs but lacks tangible recognition.
insights.I4: Skill growth is rewarded via certifications, promotions, or bonuses.

questions.question: How confident is the leadership team in navigating digital transformation?

options.O1: Leadership lacks exposure or interest in digital topics.
options.O2: We are learning but rely heavily on external advisors.
options.O3: Leaders are informed and take active interest in initiatives.
options.O4: Leadership drives transformation directly with clarity and conviction.

insights.I1: Leadership is skeptical or passive toward digital transformation.
insights.I2: Leaders are curious but rely on external support or vendors.
insights.I3: Leaders are invested in the process and review initiatives periodically.
insights.I4: Leadership drives transformation directly and leads from the front.

questions.question: Who drives decisions around adopting new tools or processes?

options.O1: Only the CEO or owner makes such decisions.
options.O2: Decisions are taken after vendor consultations.
options.O3: Senior leadership team jointly evaluates and decides.
options.O4: Cross-functional teams are empowered to propose and drive decisions.

insights.I1: All decisions made by one person; no systemized evaluation process.
insights.I2: Decisions driven by vendor advice or personal intuition.
insights.I3: Leadership team collectively makes informed tech decisions.
insights.I4: Cross-functional teams evaluate needs and recommend solutions.

questions.question: How do you approach hiring or onboarding for digital roles?

options.O1: We avoid hiring due to lack of clarity or budget.
options.O2: We hire when forced, usually based on references.
options.O3: We look for digital readiness in key roles when hiring.
options.O4: We proactively plan digital talent needs and source accordingly.

insights.I1: Avoid hiring for digital roles due to unclear needs or cost fears.
insights.I2: Reactive hiring with generic profiles, limited digital mindset.
insights.I3: Hiring includes digital mindset evaluation, but limited outreach.
insights.I4: Proactive planning for tech roles through colleges, freelancers, or networks.

questions.question: How do employees react to new technology or process changes?

options.O1: Mostly resistant, fearing workload or job loss.
options.O2: Some openness, but lots of hesitation.
options.O3: Employees are receptive if changes are explained clearly.
options.O4: Teams are proactive and often suggest improvements themselves.

insights.I1: Employees fear tech; believe automation will replace them.
insights.I2: Resistance is common; teams are hesitant to adopt changes.
insights.I3: Employees accept changes when rationale is communicated well.
insights.I4: Employees are active participants in suggesting improvements.

questions.question: How do you manage employee concerns during digital transformation?

options.O1: We don’t communicate much unless there’s resistance.
options.O2: We give updates but rarely engage in dialogue.
options.O3: We explain changes and gather feedback from time to time.
options.O4: We involve employees early and treat change as a shared journey.

insights.I1: No formal way to address employee concerns; change met with fear.
insights.I2: Minimal feedback mechanisms; top-down communication prevails.
insights.I3: Feedback is gathered through town halls or manager check-ins.
insights.I4: Employees are involved from planning to rollout; co-creation encouraged.

questions.question: Is there a culture of continuous learning in your company?

options.O1: Not really; people focus only on core jobs.
options.O2: Some self-driven learners exist, not company-wide.
options.O3: Management encourages learning but uptake is mixed.
options.O4: Learning is ingrained – people share knowledge and explore new tools.

insights.I1: Learning not prioritized; employees focus only on routine tasks.
insights.I2: Curious individuals learn on their own but lack support.
insights.I3: Company encourages learning, but no structured tracking exists.
insights.I4: Learning culture is strong—peer learning and idea sharing are routine.

questions.question: How comfortable are older and younger employees in working together on digital tasks?

options.O1: There’s a visible gap and lack of collaboration.
options.O2: They work separately but respect each other’s domains.
options.O3: We encourage collaboration through informal pairing.
options.O4: We have structured mentorships or task forces for intergenerational learning.

insights.I1: Visible friction between older and younger employees on tech usage.
insights.I2: They tolerate each other’s styles, but don't collaborate much.
insights.I3: Some informal pairing helps reduce the tech generation gap.
insights.I4: We have structured mentoring for mutual learning across generations.
